content:
Disclaimer : since I'm giving a name and maybe somewhat praising it, it might sound like an ad. But it is not, I have never paid for any AI in my life, and I don't think I ever will. This is a free tool that -to my surprise- did the task pretty well. I will never recommend paying for any AI tool including this one. While I did find it useful, I'd be happy if it died.
This morning I was watching a devlog on youtube. As pretty much all dev youtubers do, the guy was using an ✨AI powered✨ IDE, called antigravity. I liked its interface, and decided to check its website and saw that it said "free unlimited autocompletions", I said "what the hell, I'll give it a shot"
And realized what I've been missing out on.
I never did - and never will - give an AI a large task like writing a whole feature. But I asked it to clean up some of my controllers, move some logic into separate files, make the toast alert look better. It did all of them pretty perfectly. Although I'm gonna disable the autocomplete feature because it just can't make up its mind and keeps changing a line that it just edited.
Here are some of my rules :

• I do the thinking, I set up the database structure, I decide what goes where

• No more than 3-5 file changes at once, depending on the scale of the changes

• I read every line of code generated, and change it if/when necessary

• Boring stuff only. While I am a full stack developer, I'm mainly backend. So the less time I spend building a form UI, the better. It's deterministic (I have pre-made css classes and layout schema for forms), tedious, and boring. Perfect to offload to someone/something else.
For that boring stuff, so far I've been using web versions of various free tier AIs. Like deepseek, grok, and gemini. I don't use chatgpt because they heavily nerfed their free tier recently and you run out of tokens after like three messages.
The "agentic" method, for me at least, is just an easier alternative to copy-paste code back and forth between the IDE and browser. I've been a web developer for 15 years, and at this point it's just nice not having to do all the boring parts of my job. But I will actively try not to depend heavily on it since we all know what's gonna happen to free tier AI tools, any minute now.
However, I can only recommend this or similar tools to experienced developers. Because you should always be in control, it only takes ONE mistake to make it compound and turn into an unusable mess. You should always know what's being done on your project, and know enough about programming to stop your clanker and get your hands dirty when necessary.
We've been feeding google money and data for years. Why not this time we take advantage of them for a change.

  submitted by   /u/mekmookbro
[link]   [comments]
